data.title: The London Company Small Cap Vs. Russell 2000 Full Year 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Full Year Market Update

U.S. equities posted strong gains in 2025, marking a third consecutive year of double-digit returns, with the Russell 3000 Index rising 17.2%. The year was shaped by robust earnings growth, continued AI enthusiasm driving technology leadership, and incrementally more supportive monetary policy. However, the market navigated significant volatility, particularly during the spring tariff uncertainty when the S&P 500 fell nearly 19% before recovering. The Federal Reserve reduced rates by 75 basis points in the second half of the year, responding to moderating growth and signs of labor market softening while inflation remained above target. Trade policy uncertainty and tariff developments contributed to periodic volatility, though markets generally proved resilient as implementation timelines shifted.

Like recent years, equity market leadership was again concentrated in Large Cap Growth companies, particularly within technology-oriented areas tied to artificial intelligence, as the equal-weight S&P 500 meaningfully trailed its cap-weighted counterpart. Stylistically, Growth led Value for the year, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Sector performance was mixed, with cyclical leadership led by Communication Services, Technology, and Industrials, while more defensive areas lagged. Among market factors, Growth, Volatility, and Size factors posted the strongest relative returns. Momentum also helped. The Quality and Yield factors were large headwinds. The Value factor was mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

The London Company Small Cap portfolio returned 0.0% (-1.0% net) year-to-date vs. a 12.8% increase in the Russell 2000 Index. Both sector exposure and stock selection were headwinds to relative performance.

Our Small Cap portfolio underperformed for the year and came up short of our upside participation expectations. The full year report card may be disappointing, but it masks the notable developments and context beneath the surface. All the relative weakness occurred between April and October. A historically strong rally in unprofitable, high-beta stocks drove market gains down cap, which our Quality-focused, Lower-Volatility portfolio did not fully capture. Additionally, the portfolio faced sector headwinds, and pockets of idiosyncratic weakness weighed on performance. That said, the portfolio outperformed in Q1 and Q4. It played strong defense amid the volatility early in the year, and it gained ground in the final months as high beta lost traction.

In our view, this is a market where discipline matters more than bold forecasts, and portfolios should be prepared for a wider range of possible outcomes. We believe our Quality-at-a-Reasonable-Price approach may offer participation in market upside while providing the differentiation that may matter most if volatility increases or leadership shifts.

Past performance should not be taken as a guarantee of future results.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) – AWI shares outperformed for the year as they continue to operate well, employing key initiatives to offset a choppy market outlook and low volumes. We continue to like AWI for its consistent execution, strong financials, leading market share and persistent moats through its exclusivity agreements and warranties.

Somnigroup International Inc. (SGI) – SGI continues to perform well despite weak industry demand. Approval of the Mattress Firm acquisition was a major development, providing long-term benefits from vertical integration and materially strengthening the company's competitive position, with early synergies already evident. The combination has the potential to unlock meaningful value, and our thesis is further supported by strong free cash flow generation, brand equity, and solid management execution. We had to exit the name given the market cap over $14B, but continue to hold it in other portfolios.

NewMarket Corporation (NEU) – NEU has been a top performer this year, driven by strong results in its newly acquired defense businesses and stronger profitability in its core business. Continued strength in its rocket business is led by further global conflicts and stockpile replenishment. Finally, the company has been using cash flow to repay outstanding debt, which the market is viewing positively.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

Interparfums, Inc. (IPAR) – IPAR underperformed as a weaker consumer environment slowed the fragrance market, prompting cautious retailer inventory management and near-term destocking. As a result, management has lowered its outlook into 2026. Despite these headwinds, we remain confident in IPAR's ability to deliver above-market growth over time, supported by category expansion, premiumization, and strong brand execution.

Enovis Corporation (ENOV) – ENOV underperformance reflected slower-than-expected growth in orthopedic implants, along with tariff headwinds, acquisition integration costs, and a CEO transition. Despite these near-term pressures, the orthopedic implant portfolio continues to deliver healthy growth and gain market share, while demand for the company's industry-leading bracing products remains resilient. We are confident that ENOV's attractive and diversified orthopedic portfolio supports a long runway for continued earnings growth.

Landstar System, Inc. (LSTR) – LSTR has underperformed this year as the prolonged freight recession, excess capacity, and weak demand continue to pressure volumes and margins across trucking. While near-term conditions remain challenging, the company is focused on cost discipline and operational stabilization, and we believe a normalization in freight demand would highlight the earnings power of LSTR's asset-light, variable cost business model.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Energy (a weaker-performing sector) & overweight Industrials (a better-performing sector)
  • What Hurt: Underweight Health Care (a better-performing sector) & overweight Consumer Staples (a weaker-performing sector)

Portfolio Characteristics & Positioning

We believe the Small Cap portfolio is positioned for long-term durability and possesses the fundamental ingredients that stand the test of time: wide moats, sustainably high returns on capital, strong cash flow generation, low leverage ratios, and trading at a reasonable valuation. As a corporate debt maturity wall approaches and the cost of that capital stays elevated, we believe companies with strong balance sheets and the ability to self-fund their operations could have a structural advantage in 2026 and beyond. In an environment of possibly lower returns and greater volatility, we believe the Small Cap portfolio offers an attractive option for equity investors.

Looking Ahead

As we move into 2026, the economic and policy backdrop remains characterized by a mix of support and uncertainty. On the positive side, corporate earnings trends have remained better than expected, consumer activity has shown resilience, and ongoing Fed rate cuts combined with fiscal stimulus measures may continue to support growth. At the same time, late-cycle dynamics are becoming more evident. Labor market softening, affordability pressures, and persistent housing weakness underscore the uneven nature of growth. Trade and tariff policy remain fluid, and elevated complacency leaves markets vulnerable to adverse surprises. Against this backdrop, economic and inflation data may remain volatile, increasing the likelihood of episodic market dislocations. Despite resilient earnings trends and elevated hyperscaler capital spending, we believe caution is prudent given these crosscurrents.

From an equity market perspective, strong headline returns have masked growing dispersion beneath the surface. Leadership has remained narrow and valuation risk increasingly concentrated, raising the possibility that company-specific risks could take on broader significance. Periodic risk-on rallies, particularly among high-beta and lower-quality segments, have contributed meaningfully to recent performance, but history suggests such episodes are rarely durable. We believe the environment is becoming more conducive to broader leadership and a return to fundamentals, where earnings growth, dividends, and balance-sheet strength matter more than valuation multiple expansion. As volatility rises, downside protection is likely to be driven by companies with high and stable returns on invested capital, conservative leverage, and reasonable valuations. Our portfolios remain anchored in these attributes, which we believe offer a more resilient path through shifting market regimes and help clients participate in long-term wealth creation while managing downside risk.

data.title: AI Is Rocket Fuel For Duolingo
data.description:
Duolingo (DUOL) is currently is a very fortunate position. When most companies talk about AI transformation, they are mainly talking about how AI makes their internal tooling more efficient. In Duolingo's case, AI completely changes the unit-economics of the product itself of making lessons, improving lessons, and scaling lessons into entirely new subjects and domains.

Duolingo does not just benefit from AI internally. Rather, it offers a product which can continually absorb the rapid progress of the latest frontier models with minimal refactors. It also allows the company increase the rate of compounding in which it can improve its own product, through data, iteration speed, and a learning flywheel.

Duolingo's Habit-Building and Size Advantage

Duolingo is now a clear dominant leader in the global gamified learning industry. The company boasts tens of millions of daily users and over 100 million monthly active users. This scale matters because even small improvements in course quality translate to millions of extra practice sessions. These millions of extra practice sessions can then turn into signals in which Duolingo can and does use for improved personalization and teaching.

AI is a Catalyst For Fast Course Creation

To say that AI is beneficial to Duolingo's business model is an understatement. Historically, the largest costs associated with edtech was the human labor associated with created high-quality and tailored course content. AI allows Duolingo to create high-quality content at far faster pace by treating it as an engineering problem: Course experts define course guardrails and goals, and AI helps generate these courses at a fraction at a cost and at far greater speed.

From the consumer's perspective, AI allows Duolingo to address many of the bottlenecks that have plagued the company. Most notably, AI's ability to allow for conversational role-play can vastly improve the language learning experience. Another example is the Explain My Answer feature, which can address issues at the exact moment of confusion. Of course, integrating state-of-the-art AI costs money, however this is offset by the obvious benefits it confers. For instance, Duolingo's Q3 gross margin was still 72.5%, as the management addressed the slight YoY decline to generative AI and hosting costs, while expanding profitability in other areas and improving the product for long-term stickiness.

Birdbrain is Duolingo's Secret Weapon

Despite all the attention on the latest frontier AI models from the likes of OpenAI or Anthropic, Duolingo's in-house personalization model Birdbrain is its real secret weapon. Birdbrain is built to know how difficult a lesson will likely be for an individual and how they should proceed at the right pace.

This is incredibly important as striking the right balance of difficulty for a learner is critical for consumer stickiness. The hard part of this is deciding exactly which exercises are right for the consumers in their current journey. Duolingo's massive scale gives Birdbrain far more chances to figure this formula out that competitors can realistically match. The company is clearly leaning into the scale advantage as it report R&D expenses of $82.7 million in Q3 as it pours resources into the ML infrastructure that makes Birdbrain possible.

The Wrapper Advantage

Unlike many of the doomed players in the AI race, Duolingo does not need to play the frontier model game. All the company needs to do is effectively translate frontier-model capabilities into a delightful customer learning experience and learn/iterate at a faster rate than competitors.

The company has taken advantage of rapidly improving AI capabilities in the premium-tier, and then eventually transferring these advantages to its free tier as models get cheaper over time. While this strategy is far from free, particularly for richer features like conversations, the economics of token consumption are improving at an incredibly fast pace. Duolingo has already proven that it is economical to buy inference at scale, evident in its 29.5% adjusted EBITDA margin in Q3.

Duolingo's Superior Approach

Duolingo's gamified has often been mocked as unserious. However, the more sophisticated interpretation is that Duolingo is actually building a far more effective learning system that optimizes well-researched areas that traditional instruction fails miserably: frequent retrieval practice, immediate feedback, and personalized pacing.

Duolingo's style shines even more with advancing AI capabilities, where the long-lasting criticism of Duolingo's teachings not translating to real-world conversation no longer apply. As was previously mentioned, Duolingo's increasingly adaptive courses, which include conversations and other real-world like scenarios, offer a far greater learning experience. If you believe that AI is trending towards ever-more accurate voice and conversation modes, which is a basically an inevitability at this point, then Duolingo's "game loop" should be able to provide far greater value at scale.

Transforming into a Learning Platform: Beyond Languages

Duolingo has always been seen as just a "language app," which is surprising considering that fact that even its name suggests this to be the case. However, the company is rapidly transitioning to a broader consumer learning ecosystem, again made possible by the rapid advances of AI. The company has successfully expanded its ecosystem to popular subjects like music, math, and chess, while keeping its effective habit loop, game layer, and personalized infrastructure.

If Birdbrain and Duolingo's personalization, every new subject acts as a startup that just compounds on top of Duolingo's growing infrastructure. This kind of optionality is incredibly rare and becomes far more valuable when AI significantly lowers the cost of building high-quality content.

The Flywheel Investment Thesis

The most bullish way to view Duolingo is as a machine that is running a compounding loop where AI increases the slope of improvement and the ability to learn from the learners. In other words, AI makes it far cheaper to generate high-quality content and far richer courses. The data generated from these courses make it easier to personalize, sequence, and retain. These make for a flywheel that make it incredibly difficult for competitors to replicate.

Basically, the flywheel looks something like this. More learners translate to more outcome signals, which translates into better personalization. This improved personalization translates to greater retention, learning outcomes, and even referrals. All of this translates into more revenue in which Duolingo can fund even more product investments, thus strengthening and restarting the loop.

This is made all the more powerful by the fact that education is one of the few areas where even minor product improvements can be measured in learning outcomes and not just some vague engagement metrics.

Risks

The biggest risk facing Duolingo is the near-term reputational damage it can take if it integrates AI poorly. This means that Duolingo has to be incredibly vigilant with the guardrails and prompts they integrate. This risk is especially pronounced as they scale up their AI integrations. Already, we have seen major public backlash regarding Duolingo's use of AI. Many users have reported dissatisfaction with the use of AI in their courses. While this is to be expected in the early stages of such integration, Duolingo still needs to be mindful of AI potential pitfalls if not implemented in a thoughtful manner.

The second major risk to Duolingo is margin pressure from its use of AI. Every AI interaction costs Duolingo in inference costs. In fact, in a released inference customer report from OpenAI, Duolingo was actually the company's top token consumer. If Duolingo does not carefully monitor its token usage, costs could easily spiral out of control. In fact, management already the 2025 margin impact AI as inevitable, but also stated that they were measurable and bounded. FY 2025 experienced a gross margin down by about 80 bps YoY, which much of this directly related to AI spend.

Conclusion

Duolingo's potential comes from the clean fit of what AI is particularly good and what effective gamified products need. Duolingo's years of experience with rapid content iteration, feedback-driven optimization, and personalization make it an increasingly valuable tool with AI acting as a major tailwind. At a market capitalization of $5B and a forward P/E of just 14, Duolingo still has far more room to run.

data.title: Columbia Overseas Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

Another quarter, another positive global equity market. For the fourth quarter, developed markets ex-U.S. (per the MSCI EAFE Index) were up 6.13% in local currency terms, although relative strength in the U.S. dollar detracted 1.27% for U.S.-based investors, lowering their return to 4.86%. The full-year 2025 return was 20.60% in local currency and 31.22% in U.S. dollars. Supportive fiscal and monetary policies around the world and the ongoing expectation that policy will continue to be stimulative propelled markets higher during the fourth quarter. Plunging oil prices also contributed to the belief that inflation will stay contained and therefore policy settings can remain favorable. Precious metals prices exploded during the quarter, with gold, silver and platinum up 11.15%, 49.53% and 25.48%, respectively. Interestingly, Bitcoin, often viewed as an alternative to fiat currency similar to precious metals, was actually down 23.55%. Volatility in the equity market was well contained, although bond market volatility, represented by the Merrill Lynch Option Volatility Estimate (MOVE) index, continued to fall toward historic lows.

From a country perspective, all developed markets were positive during the quarter, with the exception of Australia, when measured in local currency terms. Austria, Finland and Ireland led the way, up 17.97%, 14.32% and 14.29%, respectively. Australia was the worst-performing developed market, down 1.57%, with New Zealand up 0.58% and Portugal up 0.74%. On a sector basis, utilities and health care were the standout performers, up 10.63% and 10.39% in local currencies, followed by financials, up 8.37%. Communication services was the biggest laggard and the only sector with a negative return, down 5.01%, followed by real estate, up 2.59%, and consumer discretionary, up 3.62%.

Quarterly portfolio recap

From a sector perspective, security selection drove the fund's relative underperformance, particularly within the consumer discretionary, consumer staples and information technology sectors. Security selection contributed within industrials, real estate and materials. An overweight to communication services contributed, while an overweight to energy detracted.

In country terms, positioning in the United Kingdom, Israel and Greece detracted. Positioning in Ireland, Canada and Australia contributed.

Quarterly contributors to absolute and relative results

  • Canadian silver miner Pan American Silver jumped in value as the stock took part in a standout quarter for silver and other precious metals.
  • Italian bank BPER Banca rose in price as it delivered a well-received earnings report, helped by the integration of its acquisition of a regional competitor.
  • Global biopharma company Jazz Pharmaceuticals spiked on a positive trial result for a key pipeline drug.

Quarterly detractors to absolute and relative results

  • Greek bank Piraeus modestly pulled back after the stock nearly doubled over the first nine months of the year.
  • Despite posting decent results in November, Japanese retail-drug-store operator MatsukiyoCocokara continued a decline that began after a letdown earnings report in August. Investors also anticipated that strained China-Japan relations could affect short-term performance.
  • British litigation finance provider Burford Capital posted a disappointing earnings report amid uncertainty in the company's ongoing legal battle with the country of Argentina.

Outlook

Looking forward, we expect the economic backdrop characterized by slow, steady growth to continue, implying the same steady trajectory for aggregate earnings growth. Therefore, trends in markets are likely to be driven by monetary and fiscal policy decisions, which provide the liquidity backdrop that often determines the multiple ascribed to those earnings. Policy settings were largely supportive during the second half of 2025; given the stabilization in inflation trends, even where above target, we see those favorable policy settings continuing as we enter 2026. We believe it will be the politics of inflation (more precisely, the post-COVID jump in the cost of living) that will ultimately constrain the ability of policymakers to continue fiscal and monetary policy support and therefore mark the end of this incredible post-COVID bull market.

data.title: Buenaventura: Trifecta Of Gold, Copper And Silver
data.description:
Introduction

As mentioned in my initial analysis of Compañía de Minas Buenaventura S.A.A. (BVN), it had been on my radar for over 20 years, and I finally bought into the shares last August 2025 after meeting with the company and confirming a structural change in management/ownership with a focus on growth and capital discipline. BVN had suffered from decades long sub-par engineering, business management that culminated in a massive back tax bill and closed mine that nearly broke the company. New management has entered and greatly streamlined operations, paid down debt, and brought the company back to benefit from the current metal price increases. Today, I update estimates that point to even better results given the metal price levels. I raise the YE26 price target to $52 from $27.

BVN 4Q25 Earnings Preview

BVN has scant analyst coverage; therefore, all estimates are my own. I did not have an opportunity to discuss 4Q25 production or guidance with the company and assumed the average volume seen so far this year, along with costs. What has changed is input prices, which, for all of its main metals, have risen sharply since mid-2025 and more so than in 4Q24. It should not come as a surprise to many that BVN reports a nearly 200% increase in EBITDA and a 5x increase in net income. What will be more important in the 4Q25 report is the company's progress on its mine expansion.

Operating Model

The company provides per mine data that allows for operating granularity, but I opted to model on a per metal basis: gold, silver, and copper. In the recent investor day presentation, the company provided detailed volume production and ore grade guidance for most of its mines.

The San Gabriel mine, which has a 4 oz/ton grade, started production in 4Q24 and should drive down cash costs as well as increase volumes by 40% in 2026. Silver production is expected to increase by 26% through 2028, while its own copper production is down marginally. However, the 20% stake in Cerro Verde, operated by Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) should continue to add over $300m in equity income and $150m in dividends (actual cash flow).

Financial Estimates

Given expected production and metal prices, the company should see EBITDA reach a record of over $1.5bn in 2026 and net income of nearly $1.3bn. Equity income comes from the Cerro Verde stake, which, in cash flow, is reduced by 50% to represent the cash dividends paid. I estimate over $800m in FCF, which easily funds capex and provides a massive windfall that can open the possibilities to embark on significant copper projects in JV with Antofagasta Minerals (ANFGF), which acquired a 19% stake in BNV at the end of 2023.

BVN Stock Valuation

As with all the other mining stocks I cover, I have moved the valuation parameters from a P/CE (cash earnings or net income, depreciation, and stock-based-compensation) vs. growth or a PEG ratio, to a fixed EV/EBITDA multiple. This is due to the complexity of estimating peer cash earnings given the many minority holdings and JVs that mining companies have, as well as due to the volatility of commodity prices that make longer-term relative growth ratios useless.

BVN has traded at a premium EV/EBITDA multiple to many larger-cap gold miners, primarily due to its Cerro Verde (copper mine) stake, which anchored cash flow and earnings. Now that silver is becoming a very large part of EBITDA and has a higher valuation than gold, most likely due to the industrial demand mechanics of silver vs. pure store of value for gold, I have used a 7x adjusted EV/EBITDA multiple. This incorporates the Cerro Verde income and reduces the minority interest in consolidated mines.

My YE26 price target of $51.8 is far higher than the consensus of $30.9 and is most likely due to analysts not yet updating for current or higher metal prices. The implied EV/EBITDA of 4x is simply too low, in my view.

Peer Comps

Given that BVN has almost equal parts gold, silver, and copper, I compared its growth, margins, and valuation to mining companies of these three metals. However, pure silver miners almost do not exist, as discussed in the First Majestic (AG) analysis. Nonetheless, it seems clear that copper and silver command far higher multiples than gold, perhaps due to the industrial demand that helps lend an anchor for the market, i.e., estimate a supply/demand balance. While gold is useless for industrial applications and depends on humanity's psychology, where we have deemed it valuable for its color and scarcity, this makes demand less predictable. BVN could see its multiples expand above the 7x EV/EBITDA as copper and silver overpower gold. As a pure gold company, BVN is quite junior and would garner a discounted valuation in my view. Barrick (B) is a valuation outlier given its size, and this may be due to high jurisdiction risk.

Risk

Mining is a high-risk business that can be harmed by mine closures caused by accidents, strikes, or social disruption. It is also a high fixed cost model, and any decline in metal prices results in lower margins and earnings. BVN operations are in Peru and thus have added concentration risk, albeit Peru is a highly business-friendly jurisdiction for mining.

Conclusion

I rate BVN a buy: The sharp gains in metal prices are not reflected in valuations or consensus data. In addition, BVN may garner increased valuation as copper and silver increase their EBITDA contribution. Finally, the substantial increase in FCF may allow the company to execute large growth projects, perpetuating shareholder value.

data.title: CoreWeave: Too Many Red Flags Before Q4 Earnings
data.description:
CRWV Stock: FQ4 earnings expected on Feb. 26

CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV) share prices have fallen significantly lately and may tempt some investors to do some bottom fishing here. If you feel this way, then this article is for you. My goal in this article is to A) caution investors about many red flags I am seeing with the stock under current conditions and thus B) urge potential investors to stay on the sideline till the company provides more clarity on these issues in its upcoming FQ4 earnings report (ER) scheduled on Feb. 26 (see the snapshot below for more details).

In the remaining sections of this article, I will detail the top concerns on my list, including debt increase, funding sustainability, high valuation risks, and also competition intensification, judging by the FQ4 updates provided by hyperscalers (some of which are CRWV’s patterners and some of its competitors). These confluences of financial and operational risks warrant extreme investor caution, in my view, as detailed below.

CRWV’s funding sustainability brought into question

Reading its recent financial statements, I have become quite concerned over the company’s funding and debt levels. I suspect the situation will become worse in FQ4 and will keep pressuring the share prices even more after the release of FQ4 ER. As all potential investors already know, one of CRWV’s largest customers is OpenAI, which has recently attracted questions about its funding roadmap. OpenAI is yet to find a sustainably profitable business model in my view but has been in constant need of extremely high capital requirements to build out artificial intelligence infrastructure thus far.

With CRWV’s high degree of reliance on OpenAI, my concern for CRWV's funding sustainability is even higher than for OpenAI. Here I will cite a few figures to illustrate my concern. As you can see from its balance sheet below, in just the past three months, CoreWeave has added almost $3 billion of long-term debt to its balance sheet ($10.3 billion by the end of FQ3 vs. $7.4 billion by the end of FQ2, 2025). Total liabilities surged even more, by about $6.6 billion (to $29.0 billion by the end of FQ3 vs. $22.4 billion by the end of FQ2, 2025).

In tandem with the debt increase, interest costs have soared from $267 million in FQ2 to more than $310 million in FQ3, as shown in its income statement below. Assuming FQ2’s interest costs, the annualized total interest expense would exceed $1.2 billion. To better contextualize things, the company’s operating income was only $51.9 million in the past quarter. Even its 2025 total revenue is projected to be only $5.11 billion, which makes my projected interest expense almost a quarter of its REVENUE. Looking ahead, I see no clear path for the company to earn a net profit, and neither does consensus, as you can see from the second table below. Consensus expects negative EPS for the next 2 fiscal years and an EPS of $2.17 in FY3, which translates into a lofty FWD P/E of about 44x.

FQ4 updates from key patterners and competitors

Besides my concerns about the company’s own financials and profitability outlook, the latest updates from CRWV’s key patterners and competitors in their FQ4 ER also suggest to me the shifting competitive dynamics in the AI space. The shifts are not in CRWV’s favor, as I see. CoreWeave is currently aligned closely with OpenAI and also Microsoft. On the positive side, these patterns still have a solid market share. However, other companies have been evolving and advancing their AI offerings quickly. Alphabet’s Gemini 3 is a notable example. My assessment based on MSFT and GOOG’s FQ4 ER is that Gemini 3 has been rapidly gaining share and surpassed other solutions on capability metrics. As a reflection, you can see how GOOG and MSFT stock prices diverged substantially in the 4th quarter of 2025. Google does not use CoreWeave’s data centers and does not have any financing issues for reasons detailed in one of my recent articles titled “S&P 500: AI CAPEX Reality Check.” I also believe that Google has the potential for higher margins thanks to the synergies offered by its highly popular Chrome internet browser.

Other risks and final thoughts

Despite the above risks and opacity of profitability outlook, the stock is priced with extreme multiples. As aforementioned, the stock has no meaningful earnings. In terms of EV/sales ratios, the stock is priced in a range of 13x to 15x, as shown in the chart below.

These valuation metrics are not only extreme by absolute standards or by the standard of the sector average (with an average EV/sales ratio around 3.2x to 3.4x only), but are also extreme even when compared to companies that have demonstrated a super balance of growth and profitability. For instance, the following McKinsey study provided statistics for the EV/sales ratio for a collection of businesses that have passed the so-called rule of 40 test (R40).

Data show that investors reward companies that are at or above the Rule of 40 with consistently higher enterprise value (EV) to revenue multiples. Moreover, the higher the number, the greater the gain. Top-quartile SaaS companies generate nearly three times the multiples of those in the bottom (exhibit).

As seen in the chart below, companies that can pass the R40—which are overachievers in my experience—have enjoyed an average valuation multiple of 12x EV/sales. For those that pass but with an R40 score in the bottom quartile, they are priced with a slightly lower EV/sales, averaging 8x. In CRWV’s case, its EV/sales ratios are quite extreme even compared to these overachievers.

To make the analysis balanced, there are certainly positives. I certainly feel positive about the secular growth potential of artificial intelligence. The large AI CAPEX budget announced by hyperscalers in their FQ4 ER underscores customers' strong or even urgent need for more computing power. Collectively, these signals highlight a potential addressable market for CoreWeave. As a reflection of such potential, CoreWeave’s revenues are projected to more than double in FY 2026 (to about $12 billion compared to about $5.1 billion for FY 2025). As another reflection, NVDA recently announced an investment in CRWV to expand their collaboration (see details quoted below):

Seeking Alpha News: Nvidia (NVDA) has invested $2B in CoreWeave (CRWV) and expanded their collaboration to help CoreWeave accelerate the buildout of over 5 gigawatts of AI factories by 2030. Nvidia invested $2B in CoreWeave Class A common stock at $87.20 per share.

Overall, my assessment is that the negatives outweigh the positives under current conditions. In particular, I want to caution potential investors to resist the urge of bottom-fishing despite the large price pullback lately. For existing investors, I recommend hedging strategies against a negative surprise in the FQ4 ER, which I see good odds for based on its own financials and operations as well as those of its close patterners.

data.title: 4 Strong High-Yield REITs For The Value Rotation
data.description:
There is clear evidence that the stock market is nearing a top. As noted by Seeking Alpha analyst Erik Conley, the Put/Call ratio is well below its long-term average of 0.70, indicating investor overconfidence, and the 10-year Treasury yield has been inching higher, despite cuts in the Fed Funds rate.

There is also clear evidence of a rotation in stocks, out of growth and into value. At times like these, investors often turn to solid assets, including real estate. REITs are the easiest way to invest in real estate since they trade like stocks.

Over the past 20 years, REITs have only been 0.56 correlated to stocks, according to NAREIT. This means that commercial real estate is a meaningful and timely diversifier of your investment portfolio.

Year-to-date, the average REIT (4.71%) is outperforming the S&P 500 (1.82%) and the NASDAQ (0.06%). However, the average high-yielding REIT (6.12%) is outperforming the REIT average. So this appears to be an excellent time to add some high-yield REITs.

The problem with high-yield investing is that often, those yields turn out to be siren songs. Generally speaking, the higher the yield, the greater the risk to the underlying capital. That is particularly true if the company cuts its dividend, because that is usually followed immediately by a sharp sell-off in share price. Thus, in choosing a high-yield investment, it is important to look first at Dividend Safety, then at balance sheet quality and forward growth. Finally, once all those screens have been applied and passed, price becomes important.

All the companies on this list yield 5.50% or higher, which is at least 200 basis points above the current 2-year Treasury yield of 3.49% at the time of this writing. All have Dividend Safety that is acceptable to good, sturdy balance sheets and positive 2-year forward revenue growth.

In the discussion that follows, numbers highlighted in Green indicate areas where the company outperforms both the sector and overall REIT averages. Numbers highlighted in Red indicate potential areas of concern, while numbers highlighted in Yellow are typical of both the company's sector and REITs overall.

Getty Realty (GTY)

Established as a REIT in 1977, Getty Realty specializes in the acquisition, financing, and development of convenience, automotive, and other single-tenant retail sites, mostly the ubiquitous gas station convenience stores and car washes. The company has 1160 properties in 44 U.S. states and rakes in $210 million ABR (annual base rent). The portfolio enjoys 99.8% occupancy.

GTY Dividends

Getty is an outstanding payer, currently yielding 6.30% with a Dividend Safety grade of C, which indicates a generous sharing of profits with the investor and no significant threat of a cut in the next 12 months. The company is also growing the dividend at a healthy rate of 4.5% over the past 3 years, resulting in a Dividend Score of 7.19.

The payout ratio of 80% is a trifle aggressive, but Getty has paid a dividend in each of the past 168 quarters, even during COVID, always the same or higher than the previous quarter.

GTY Balance Sheet Quality

Getty's balance sheet stands out for its low Debt/EBITDA ratio of 5.0. Though not bond-rated, the company's debt ratio is just 32%, and only 3.5% of the debt is held at variable rates.

VICI Properties (VICI)

Beginning in 2017 with Harrah's Las Vegas, VICI Properties has acquired a portfolio of nearly 100 gaming, hospitality, and entertainment properties, mostly casinos, spanning 31 states and totaling 127 msf (million square feet). The portfolio is 100% occupied, all with triple-net leases, bringing in over $440 million in ABR. Although the company has only 13 tenants, the WALT (weighted average lease term) is 40 years, with the shortest being 28.7.

VICI Dividends

VICI pays a handsome 6.19% dividend on a conservative 60% payout ratio and is growing the dividend faster than the average REIT, at 5.6% over the past 3 years. This results in an outstanding Dividend Score of 7.29, and the dividend is quite safe at B+.

Conclusion

In this article, I have presented 4 sturdy candidates for high-yield investing for your further investigation. With the rotation to value we are currently witnessing, this appears to be an opportune time to invest in safe, high-yield REITs, with a good chance the stock price will hold even or climb. All are rated as Buys by at least two well-regarded sources.

But that's just my opinion. As always, the opinion that matters most is yours. Because it's your money.

data.title: PDD Holdings: This Retail Disruptor Appears Undervalued
data.description:
Context

Since PDD Holdings (PDD) was last covered (HOLD rating), the stock is up over 70% despite facing an onslaught of challenges in both their home market as well as internationally. In China, they are battling a price war against eCommerce rivals including Alibaba, JD.com, and Meituan (so brutal that the government stepped in this month to stabilize extreme competitive pressures). In the U.S. and soon in Europe as well, the removal of the de minimis exemption has dramatically reduced their price advantage against established rivals, impacting user numbers and therefore GMV. Investors jitters over challenging operating conditions appear to have pushed their stock price into discount territory.

Business and market context

PDD Holdings is a Chinese eCommerce retailer. They operate two main platforms: Pinduoduo in China and Temu internationally. Founded just over a decade ago, Pinduoduo was a relative latecomer to China’s mature eCommerce market; however, their focus on ultra-low prices enabled them to capture a share of nearly 20% at the expense of established eCommerce incumbents Alibaba and JD.com. Their ability to deliver unrivaled savings was due to a strategy that included leveraging WeChat to encourage customers to make group purchases and avail themselves of group discounts (which not only increased volume and cut logistics expenses but also reduced customer acquisition thanks to word of mouth referrals by customers themselves), integrating social and gamification elements that offered a “Costco + Disneyland” shopping experience, as well as a relatively asset light strategy that enabled rapid scale up.

Temu was founded less than five years ago in 2022 and has already expanded to at least 70 countries, of which the U.S. is their biggest market. Like Pinduoduo, Temu entered a highly established market dominated by Amazon (AMZN), but again, their focus on ultra-low cost, partly driven by the de minimis tax exemption, enabled them to capture a significant share (nearly a fifth); however, that share has experienced fluctuations with the U.S. removing that exemption effective July 2027.

Financial context

Revenues are generated through two main segments with revenue share roughly at 50% each:

  • Online marketing services and others: This segment matches product listings to search results on the browser or PDD’s respective platform, for which PDD charges marketing service fees from the merchant based on impressions or clicks (fees for which can vary depending on service provided). This segment also provides display marketing services, typically provided on a fixed-fee basis.
  • Transaction services: This segment generates revenue from fulfillment services and commissions on sales made on their platforms.

While PDD Holdings has for years enjoyed robust double-digit top-line growth (over 20% YoY), the company experienced a noticeable slowdown in 2025 (revenues rose 10% YoY in Q1 2025, 7% in Q2 2025, 9% in Q3 2025, and up 9% for the first nine months of 2025). Margins contracted as well, with operating margins shrinking to 21% in 9M 2025 from 29% and net margins down to 24% from 30%, largely driven by an increase in COGS, which increased from 37% of revenues to 43% of revenues.

The slowdown was attributed to increasing competitive pressures, as well as investments in supply chain optimization in an effort to improve long-term competitiveness.

Prospects

PDD Holdings is going through a rough patch in their key China and U.S. markets, and it remains to be seen when the situation will improve. There are, however, reasons to be optimistic that PDD can withstand the storm and remain among the last ones standing.

In China, group buying pioneer Pinduoduo still remains the leader in this space despite repeated efforts by Alibaba (BABA) and JD.com (JD) to carve out a share. Pinduoduo also expanded this group buying business model to the grocery space (through a new grocery community group buying arm called Duoduo Maicai), beating out Meituan (MPNGF) and emerging as the leader in this space as well, reflecting the resilience of their business model. Meituan closed down their group community buying operations (Meituan Youxuan) in the middle of last year, and Alibaba closed theirs (Taocaicai) earlier than that.

Amid the ongoing price war, while Alibaba, JD.com, and other rivals are busy fighting battles on many fronts from legacy eCommerce to quick commerce and local services, Pinduoduo has largely remained disciplined, focusing on defending their group buying market with a sharpened focus on the ultra-low-cost segment. Their RMB 10 billion subsidy program, while not as large as Alibaba’s RMB 50 billion program, could still go a long way towards defending their turf thanks to this disciplined focus.

Moreover, Pinduoduo wields a marketing advantage in that the platform is integrated with China’s ubiquitous WeChat messaging app, owned by Tencent (TCEHY), which remains a major shareholder in PDD Holdings. This integration facilitates ‘social commerce’ deal sharing among Pinduoduo users to secure discounts, a feature that is not as prominent in rivals like Meituan (which tends to position itself as an instant commerce player rather than a social commerce player) and Alibaba (Alibaba and Tencent are arch rivals, and Tencent exited their stakes in rival Meituan).

When the dust settles and competitive pressures ease, Pinduoduo likely can take share from the vacuum left behind by casualties but also potentially improve their take rate as well, thereby helping recover margins to their former levels.

Over in the U.S., Temu’s competitive position has eroded significantly due to the removal of the de minimis exemption. The general consensus is that they are adapting via a ‘semi-managed’ wholesale model, where merchants ship products directly to U.S.-based warehouses. While this model entails higher tariff, warehousing, and fulfillment costs—potentially challenging their status as the lowest-cost retailer—Temu still has avenues to maintain its edge and possibly claw back market share lost during the height of the de minimis uncertainty.

Temu and Amazon have completely different business models. Amazon is a marketplace of sellers and buyers, similar to Alibaba and JD.com; sellers offer their wares, and buyers search and buy what they choose. Temu is the opposite in that they are more of a distributor cum retailer themselves. They control product pricing, traffic, and marketing and push product recommendations to buyers as opposed to buyers searching for products. While Amazon makes recommendations based on user interest and history, Temu makes recommendations based on the best available bargains at that moment.

Temu is heavily focused on a high-volume, low-cost model, while Amazon is more focused on higher-margin items. Towards this end, Temu’s product offering is heavily skewed towards unbranded white-label products, while Amazon’s offering tends to be brand-heavy, which naturally commands higher prices due to brand premiums. In terms of supply chain, this means Temu typically works directly with manufacturers, while Amazon works with layers of middlemen, including resellers and distributors.

Temu’s focus as a platform for bargains is amplified by their gamification elements, which make for a ‘bargain-hunting’ type shopping experience filled with thrill and surprise in contrast to Amazon, which is not positioned this way.

While Amazon’s focus is on variety, which creates its own network effects, Temu’s focus is on value deals and bargains, which has its own network effects as more bargains lure more customers, which drives higher sales volumes and therefore lower prices. It also provides a steady flow of traffic (translating into lower customer acquisition costs) as customers regularly check in to the platform to explore the latest deals. Temu’s entire algorithm is geared towards filtering out the most competitive merchants and products, recommending these to their users in front-page results, and selling as many of those products as possible to each user at the lowest possible cost and maximum possible margin. Amazon would have to tweak their entire algorithm, and while this sounds easy in that it may involve changing some code, the reality is the entire algorithm would likely have to be retrained, and if done incorrectly, it may result in negative consequences and potentially financial losses as well.

Understandably, Amazon created a new platform instead—Amazon Haul; however, it appears to be a hedge rather than a growth engine and may remain that way for as long as reasonably possible, as it is not in Amazon’s interest to cannibalize their sales with lower-value orders.

Therefore, in my view, more likely than not, the recent removal of de minimis exemptions is not likely to hinder Temu’s longer-term growth, and their current slowdown could be temporary as they adjust their supply chains to comply with a shifting regulatory environment.

Valuation

According to PDD’s annual report, substantially all of their revenues are generated in China, which means Temu’s revenue contribution is minimal. Pinduoduo generates an estimated CNY 4 trillion in GMV in China, and if we assume a 10% take rate, Pinduoduo’s revenue would roughly amount to around CNY 400 billion, which is on par with their projected revenue this year based on their current run rate (revenues amounted to CNY 307 billion for the first nine months of 2025), suggesting Temu’s revenue contribution is negligible.

To get a sense of Temu’s revenue potential, Temu earns around USD 100 million in Europe, if we assume this translates roughly into USD 300 million or so revenue and with a 10% take rate generates roughly USD 3 billion GMV, it is possible that, roughly speaking, Temu’s U.S. operations generates a GMV of around USD 3 billion or more (America’s GDP is roughly the same as Europe) which is barely 1% of Amazon’s estimated USD 400 billion GMV in the U.S. Amazon’s market share in U.S eCommerce is around 40%, which indicates Temu’s market share is not even 1%. If China’s eCommerce market is anything to go by (where Pinduoduo commands a roughly 20% market share, which was earned by taking share from established incumbents like Alibaba, which, like Amazon, holds a roughly 40% market share), a potential 20x growth (base case) medium to long term of Temu’s U.S. GMV and revenue could be possible. This would indicate additional GMV of over USD 50 billion, additional revenue of over USD 5 billion (around CNY 30 billion), and additional earnings of over USD 1.6 billion (CNY 9 billion) or so. This does not sound like much for a company with CNY 400 billion in revenues, but at this point they would have just roughly 7% market share in the U.S. and, more likely than not, considerable growth runway left. It also does not account for growth prospects elsewhere, particularly with cross-border eCommerce, where Temu, at just 4 years old, has already matched decades-old Amazon’s 24% cross-border eCommerce market share.

Despite the growth prospects, PDD Holdings trades at a very modest 10x P/E, far below the sector median of 16x, suggesting investors are overlooking PDD’s growth story or are spooked by geopolitical risks that could hinder the company’s overseas growth ambitions.

But even if we set aside their growth prospects, a P/E of 10 roughly translates into an earnings yield of around 10%, higher than their cost of equity, which suggests a very undemanding valuation even if international growth plans fall flat.

Risks

Competition

PDD Holdings competes against many established players. Douyin and Alibaba in particular stand out as formidable competitors who could potentially nibble away at Pinduoduo’s market share in their core market, China, which could result in a significant impairment to earnings and valuation.

Takeaway

PDD Holdings appears to be trading at a discount driven by market fears over geopolitical risks and price wars in their home market. The stock has a buy analyst consensus rating. I view the stock as a buy.

data.title: BETA Technologies: Amazon Surge Is A False Flag, But Vertical Integration Makes It A Buy
data.description:
BETA Technologies (BETA) stock surged 15.6% following a stake disclosure from Amazon (AMZN) and an upgrade from Jefferies. BETA Technologies is also backed by GE Aerospace (GE), and I believe that the company has a lot of unique drivers that many other developers of eVTOLs simply do not have, and that makes it more attractive for investors, including big corporations such as Amazon and GE Aerospace. In this report, I will discuss these unique drivers and assess the investment case for BETA.

BETA Technologies Amazon Surge Is A False Flag

BETA Technologies went public in November 2025, raising $1 billion in capital. BETA is already substantially different in that regard compared to peers, as it went public through a traditional IPO rather than a SPAC. Since then, the stock price has come down to as low as $15.61 before the rebound to $19.39. BETA Technologies already had quite a strong backing in investor rounds. In September 2025, GE Aerospace announced a $300 million equity investment in BETA through private placement. Amazon’s Climate Pledge Fund already invested in BETA in 2021. The stock now surged on the news that Amazon holds a stake, but this was already disclosed in the prospectus (S-1, Page 153).

We see that both Amazon and GE Aerospace were already listed in the prospectus as parties holding more than 5% ownership of the company. So, the disclosure of Amazon holding 5.3% is not a surprising one, nor does it constitute an additional equity contribution as far as I can tell. It provides investors with a sign of equity and strategic backing from Amazon, but that equity backing could already be seen in the prospectus as well as the press release from Amazon’s Climate Pledge Fund from 2021. So, in my view, it is a bit of a false flag for the company’s stock price to surge.

BETA Has An Attractive Business Setup

BETA Technologies is taking a vastly different approach, which I believe creates a lower-risk capitalization opportunity with substantially more revenue streams compared to peers. The company has two airplane products, which are the ALIA VTOL and the ALIA CTOL. In its effort to bring an eVTOL (electric vertical take-off/landing) airplane to the market, they are taking an intermediate step with the eCTOL (electric conventional take-off/landing) airplane, which is set to enter the market first and can be certified using FAA Part 23 standards, which is the certification standard for normal category airplanes. The CTOL is expected to be certified in late 2026 or early 2027, with certification for the eVTOL 12 months later. So, the company is creating two products from a somewhat unified development framework. Components such as the H500 engine are expected to be certified this year. There always is the risk of schedule delays, but BETA has a stepwise approach, certifying the engines, then the CTOL, and then the VTOL. This is an approach that is more conservative compared to peers and may also mean that the company’s revenue generation is less binary than it is for many of its peers.

The company is also positioning itself as a full-stack electric aviation OEM and propulsion merchant supplier. I believe this is a key differentiator. BETA develops and manufactures the powertrain, batteries, and charging equipment in-house, whereas most eVTOL developers are system integrators doing final assembly sourced from a pool of suppliers. This means that BETA Technologies, compared to peers, should be able to drive substantially more high-margin revenues from the aftermarket since it has the OEM (original equipment manufacturer) expertise on the component level.

Since BETA does more design and manufacturing itself, it can also provide solutions to other eVTOL manufacturers. Since BETA will also be competing with those same eVTOL manufacturers in some sense, integration with other eVTOLs may not be desired and may actually also provide some integration risk. However, overall, the company does have a vertically integrated setup along the energy/power axis of the CTOL/VTOL that will allow it to harvest aftermarket sales more efficiently, and it could also be a supplier, particularly on a unified charging network for eVTOLs.

The company also has a dual-use opportunity, meaning the product can be used for commercial as well as defense purposes. Due to the vertical setup, BETA is already generating revenues from propulsion sales even with certification not being complete yet on the component level, let alone on the aircraft level. Other revenues come from services revenues, small contributions from the charging network, and classified defense work. So, the company is already generating revenues, and we see a 70% gross profit margin. The operating loss is still $80.6 million, but it carries R&D expenses in support of development and certification of the new products.

The company also has a near-term revenue driver through eIPP, which is a government-backed integration pilot program. This could drive revenues by 2026 either from aircraft sales and battery & charging revenues or payments for demonstrations. This would provide a strong entry point for BETA to commence cargo and (medical) logistics flights. Ideally, the CTOL is certified by the time the eIPP program has a real revenue component. However, since this is a pilot program, it could be the case that uncertified airplanes will be allowed to participate under a special type certificate when a certain level of maturity is demonstrated. The earliest revenues from eIPP will not be huge, but it will be a strategically strong position to be able to participate in this program and smooth out some challenges for integration into commercial operations ahead of commercial service entry.

So, overall, the stepped approach and the energy aviation stack approach with in-house component design and manufacturing position BETA Technologies extremely well in the market.

BETA Technologies Is A Speculative Buy

Valuing BETA Technologies is not an easy task. The valuation is highly dependent on which EV/Sales multiple is used along with sales projections being correct as well as cash conversion and EBITDA margins being reasonably correct. I am currently assuming mid-point margins for reasonable margin ranges with a 4% margin for 2028, 11.5% for 2029, and 15% for 2030. We see that the stock is fully valued against an aerospace peer group multiple of 4.3x. However, we also note that emerging companies tend to trade substantially higher. At 2x the peer group multiple, we would see a 96% upside. My current stock price target is based on 1.5x peer group EV/Sales, which coincides with a 6.5x EV/Sales multiple that would indicate 48% upside with a $28.64 price target.

Conclusion: BETA Technologies Has An Extremely Attractive Setup

Names in the eVTOL landscape remain higher risk, and so any buy rating is a speculative buy rating. The buy rating for BETA Technologies is no different. I believe that the company has a very attractive setup as an eVTOL developer with OEM and energy aviation stack capabilities, enabling it to harvest high-margin aftermarket revenues from its own products but also from other parties with whom BETA has contracted for parts. I don’t believe the surge in the stock price is justified if that is driven by the Amazon stake disclosure, because that Amazon had a stake in the company was clear as day. The Jefferies upgrade provides a better reason for the increase in the stock price, and my assessment also shows that if BETA delivers on revenue expectations, substantially higher stock prices can indeed be unlocked.

data.title: VICI Properties Vs. Gaming and Leisure Properties: The Vegas-Focused REIT Is The Winner
data.description:
We recently initiated a new position in VICI Properties (VICI) and shared our investment thesis in an article that you can read by clicking here.

In short, we think that the market has overreacted to the recent slowdown in Las Vegas, not realizing that VICI is a triple net lease landlord with 40-plus-year-long leases that continued to generate steady and predictable income even through the pandemic. Even then, it's now priced at a historically low valuation, and we think that it's a great buy-the-dip opportunity.

This recent article led many of you to ask me why we don't invest in its close peer, Gaming and Leisure Properties (GLPI), instead, and that's going to be the focus of today's follow-up analysis.

After all, it's trading at an even lower valuation, offering a slightly higher yield, enjoying better diversification, and its smaller size could result in faster growth over time.

VICI GLPI
AFFO Multiple 12.1x 11.3x
Dividend Yield 6.3% 6.9%
Market Cap $31 billion $13 billion

We like GLPI, and my preference for VICI is not significant. I like casino net lease properties a lot and could see myself owning GLPI someday in the future.

You can read more about why we like casino net lease properties by clicking here.

However, the reason why we favor VICI is its greater focus on Las Vegas assets compared to GLPI, which focuses on regional properties.

VICI generates about half of its revenue from the Las Vegas Strip, owning trophy assets such as the Caesars Palace, the Venetian, the MGM Grand, etc., whereas GLPI owns regional casinos such as the Bally’s Casino in Black Hawk or DraftKings at Casino Queen in St. Louis.

VICI - the Venetian, Las Vegas, NV

GLPI - DraftKings at Casino Queen, St. Louis

Some investors argue that GLPI's regional casinos are preferable as they could be more resilient to recessions and allow the REIT to better diversify the portfolio.

That may be true, but it does not tell the full story.

Both have pros and cons, but overall, I think that the regional casinos of GLPI are riskier than the Vegas Strip casinos of VICI for three reasons:

Reason 1: Lower Barriers to Entry

The Las Vegas Strip is the most economically productive street in the entire world. Most of the land is already built out, limiting future potential supply. The properties on the strip also enjoy valuable brands, which is unique in real estate and only further increases the strength of their moats.

The same cannot be said about the regional casinos of GLPI.

They're typically in much worse locations, which puts them at greater risk of facing future competition.

Over the long run, real estate is all about location, location, location… because that’s what protects your asset from future supply and allows it to grow in value over time.

Reason 2: Greater Regulatory Risk

Las Vegas has become a global entertainment hub, attracting people from all over the world for all kinds of entertainment, not just gambling. People visit Vegas all the time to party, watch shows and sports, eat good food, relax, and then gamble a bit. The point is that Vegas does not pull its traffic just for gambling. It's well diversified, which protects it from potential regulatory changes. There's only one Vegas, and it will remain a major entertainment hub far into the future and likely only grow further over time as it keeps diversifying into new things like F1, new sports teams, the Sphere, etc.

This makes VICI's Las Vegas properties far more resilient because they enjoy a constant flow of traffic that comes to Vegas for a lot of different reasons. They then end up staying at its properties, eating out, shopping, and even gambling a bit, despite it often not being the main reason for the trip.

Regional casinos, on the other hand, exist primarily for gambling. Their entertainment options are not nearly as diversified. They're gambling destinations, and that exposes them to much greater regulatory risk. As an example, a given state may decide to increase the number of casino licenses, leading to new competition just across the street. Or it may increase its casino taxes, leading to a steep drop in profitability.

Worse yet, a nearby state may change its regulations to allow casinos in the future. I remember driving from Dallas, Texas, to just across the border to Oklahoma to visit the biggest casino in the world: The WinStar World Casino and Resort.

It's just across the border to attract gamblers from Texas, where casinos are illegal, but what if Texas were to change its regulations in the future? That casino would certainly take a big hit, as most of its visitors go there primarily for gambling and not for other forms of entertainment.

GLPI does not own this specific property, but it owns many others that are in a similar position.

As an example, GLPI owns Hollywood Casino Tunica, which is in Mississippi, right at the border with Arkansas and Tennessee, which have historically had stricter gambling rules. This casino has so far benefited from a lot of traffic coming from the neighboring states, but if their rules were to change and new competition emerged, it could lose some of this business, leading to a permanent loss in value.

This makes GLPI's regional properties a lot less resilient than the average property on the Vegas Strip. They rely primarily on gambling to attract people, and that could be disrupted in the future due to potential regulatory changes opening the door to more competitors.

Reason 3: Digital Cannibalization

Finally, I think that GLPI's regional casinos are also at higher risk of suffering from the growth of online gambling over the long run.

As mentioned earlier, Vegas will thrive because it's far more than gambling. It has become a true entertainment hub. I have gone five times to Vegas, and it was never for gambling. It has always been for sports, conferences, or just having fun with friends. While I'm there, I may gamble a little, but that’s not the primary reason why I visit Vegas. Digital gaming will not change that.

However, since regional casinos are all about gambling, I think that their traffic could be affected if more people switch to online gambling. Now, I understand that the experience is not the same, and one does not directly compete with the other. In fact, casino operators have even shown that digital gaming can benefit their physical casinos, as it introduces new people to gambling who have never set foot in a casino. By following hybrid strategies, they can bring people into their digital ecosystem, have them gamble, and then grant them rewards to be used at physical casinos to increase traffic, for instance.

This works well, and I'm not saying that GLPI's regional casinos will go under because of online gambling. But I do think that there's a long-term risk to it that cannot be overlooked.

Risks to My Thesis

If you think that Las Vegas has entered a structural and long-lasting downturn, then this would, of course, change everything. This could happen, for example, if lots of other similar destinations emerged internationally, leading to greater competition.

In that case, VICI's tenants would likely see their profitability decline, and this could eventually lead to lease defaults and even a dividend cut.

I don't expect that to happen, but if you think that the best days of Vegas are behind it, then you would likely be better off investing in GLPI instead.

Conclusion

In a way, VICI's Las Vegas casinos vs. GLPI's regional casinos are like comparing a Class A mall in a prime location with well-diversified uses, including lots of entertainment, restaurants, offices, and even apartments, to an outlet center in a more remote location with only shopping.

The outlet would be in a riskier situation because of its worse location, and its lack of diversification is exposing it to greater competitive threats from other properties and e-commerce.

This is not to say that the outlet is a bad investment, but it should trade at a discount to compensate for its higher risk.

In the case of VICI and GLPI, the discount is not large enough for me to invest in GLPI instead of VICI. I would much rather pay the small premium to own true trophy assets on the Las Vegas Strip.

Yes, this also means being more concentrated on one location, but that's worthwhile in this particular case. Similarly, I would much rather hold my assets in one bank that’s very solid rather than two weak banks. Diversification just for the sake of diversification does not always make sense.

At High Yield Landlord, we have managed to stay ahead of the REIT market averages over the long run by identifying such differences and capitalizing on those that offer the risk-to-reward to investors.

Our choice is VICI in the casino sector.

data.title: Strong Jobs Market Suggests Considering Cyclical Stocks
data.description:
The first of the labor market reports for 2026 has dropped, and it doesn't disappoint. Unemployment rate is down, non-farm payrolls are up. It doesn't get much better. Except, maybe, for rate sensitive sectors that would have benefited from economic weakness resulting in an easier monetary policy.

Interestingly though, cyclicals aren't doing too badly, and a healthy economy could well be more than sufficient to support them even if no rate cuts happen going forward. But that remains to be seen. All of this is covered in this article.

What the labor market report says

The labor market showed signs of health in January, with the unemployment rate dropping for the second consecutive month, and non-farm payroll increases did exactly the same. The details are below.

  • Unemployment rate

The unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%, which is quite the start for the year considering that it falls below the Fed's target rate of 4.4% this year. It's also the smallest rate seen in the last six months (the figure for October 2025 wasn't released).

It does bear mentioning, though, that the labor market has still weakened over the year, though. In January 2025, the unemployment rate was a notably lower 4%. Still, it's encouraging that runaway weakness hasn't happened, and the number has actually subsided from the highs of November 2025.

  • Non-farm payrolls

Non-farm payrolls increased by 130k in January 2026, the biggest monthly increase in over a year. The last time that the number expanded at an even higher rate was only in December 2024, by 237k.

Not only is the latest figure a positive on a standalone basis, but the fact that non-farm payrolls have now increased for three consecutive months (see chart below) is a relief too. Between May and October 2025, the number contracted in three of the six months.

Rate cut probability drops further

After the FOMC statement of January, the market were already fairly convinced that the Fed is unlikely to cut rates at the March 17-18 meeting. It's now even more so. The probability of a rate cut, based on the 30-day Fed funds futures rates, has dropped to just 7.5% from 13.4% after the last FOMC meeting.

That this probability has been directly impacted by the latest jobs report is evident from the fact that as of February 10, a day before its release, the probability was at a much higher 20%. Of course, it's still pre-mature to predict with certainty what might happen at the meeting, as plenty of macro data points are due before that, including another jobs report and two inflation releases. But so far, the indications of another meeting of unchanged rates is on the cards.

Stock market response

During the earnings season, it's harder to assess the impact of macroeconomic events on headline indices and the latest stock market response is no different. At yesterday's market close, the indices weren't notably moved to start with. And to the extent that they were, there wasn't harmony in their direction.

While the NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX) (QQQ) made some gains, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) was essentially flat, and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) was marginally down (see table below).

The more interesting trend is visible YTD. The Dow Jones has made the biggest gains, followed by the S&P 500 and the NASDAQ 100 is actually marginally in the red. Since the time of the previous jobs report, only DIA has made any real progress.

A year of cyclicals?

These YTD trends for key indices appear to indicate that this could well be the year of cyclicals. Consider this. The biggest five holdings for the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF Trust (DIA) are the financial services company Goldman Sachs (GS), construction and mining equipment provider Caterpillar (CAT), Microsoft (MSFT), home improvement retailer Home Depot (HD) and paints company Sherwin-Williams (SHW).

Four of these five are cyclical stocks, with Microsoft being the exception. There's more. Take a look at the chart below. Three of these five holdings have seen double digit percentage gains YTD. One, which is Goldman Sachs, has seen single digit gains, while Microsoft is the only one to have seen a double-digit percentage drop so far this year. In other words, all except the non-cyclical technology stock have seen gains in 2026 so far.

This raises the question if 2026 could well be the year of cyclicals. The latest labor market report's robustness certainly suggests so. Especially when seen in conjunction with expectations of healthy GDP growth and muted inflation this year.

A look at the sectoral trends offers no clear conclusions, at least not yet. Sectors like consumer discretionary and financial services are still weak YTD, even as others like materials and energy have seen strength, as reflected in the table below.

This leaves the question - where should an investment be made now? One option to consider would be the DIA itself. Except that the index's P/E isn't supportive, at 23.2x, as it's slightly higher than the average of 22.6x over the past five years, implying that the index could stay basically flat going forward.

A look at the ratings for DIA's biggest holdings also shows no clear buying opportunity. In fact, interestingly, Microsoft is the only one that stands out the most. Not only is it rated a Buy by Seeking Alpha analysts but also a Strong Buy by Wall Street. The point here is, right now is a rare wait and watch moment on what to buy next.

In conclusion

It might be a wait and watch, but it's a time of active waiting. Opportunities are likely to arise, if the labor markets are any indication. And most likely, they will be seen in cyclical stocks, even if no interest rate cuts happen. Even more specifically, segments like materials and industrials are worth tracking as superior performers YTD.

This could change of course, with any big policy changes or a turn in macroeconomic trends. But for now, the year appears to be tilting in favour of cyclicals.
